name: New Mexico Dept of Information Technology (DoIT)
tagline: New Mexico's central IT agency: DoIT runs enterprise services for state agencies on a cost-recovery model, houses the state's broadband office driving a $382M federal buildout, and supports the statewide cybersecurity and public-safety radio backbone.
overview: The New Mexico Department of Information Technology (DoIT) is the executive branch's central IT agency, headquartered in Santa Fe. It delivers enterprise services to state agencies — telecommunications, network and internet access, server and application hosting, the statewide SHARE ERP system, and public-safety radio (the Digital Trunked Radio System) — and is organized into six authorized programs: Program Support, Compliance and Project Management, Enterprise Services, Equipment Replacement, Broadband, and Cybersecurity. DoIT funds most of its operations through cost recovery: it charges customer agencies published rates for services rather than running on a large general-fund appropriation, and uses non-reverting equipment-replacement funds to refresh aging infrastructure. Two policy offices are administratively attached to DoIT but operate with their own mandates — the Office of Broadband Access & Expansion (OBAE), which is executing New Mexico's federal broadband buildout, and the Office of Cybersecurity (OCS), whose CISO now reports to a Cybersecurity Advisory Committee rather than to the CIO. For a Dell seller, DoIT is both an enterprise-infrastructure buyer for the state's data-center and network estate and the central authority that shapes how other agencies procure IT.

budget.intro: DoIT is funded primarily through cost recovery, not a large direct appropriation: it bills customer agencies published rates for enterprise services and sustains its non-reverting equipment-replacement funds from those fees. Its FY27 appropriation request (submitted September 2025) aligned spending to FY25 actuals and accommodated a 5% rate increase for Enterprise Services approved by the rate committee. The most consequential dollars in DoIT's orbit are pass-through and grant funds — most visibly the $382M federal broadband award flowing through OBAE.

stats.label: BEAD broadband award
stats.note: Federal NTIA/BEAD funds approved Jan 2026, administered by OBAE under DoIT

stats.label: Authorized programs
stats.note: Support, Compliance/PM, Enterprise Services, Equipment Replacement, Broadband, Cybersecurity

stats.label: Enterprise Services rate increase
stats.note: Approved by the rate committee for FY27; flows into agency IT bills

notes: DoIT recovers operating costs by charging customer agencies for telecom, hosting, network, ISP access, public-safety radio, and SHARE — so spend on Dell infrastructure shows up inside enterprise-service rates rather than a single line item.
The largest end-spending IT budgets sit with the big program agencies (HHS, Human Services, public safety); DoIT is the central provider and procurement gatekeeper, not always the wallet.
Broadband and cybersecurity dollars are heavily federal/grant-funded and time-bound — confirm the fund source and period of performance before forecasting against them.

initiatives.name: BEAD broadband buildout via OBAE
initiatives.summary: New Mexico's $382M federal BEAD plan (approved by NTIA in Jan 2026) funds 31 grants to connect 42,500+ unserved/underserved locations across 32 of 33 counties, using a tech-neutral mix of fixed wireless, fiber, and LEO satellite. OBAE, attached to DoIT, runs the program. Adjacent opportunity for network, edge, and middle-mile infrastructure rather than a direct DoIT data-center play.

initiatives.name: Statewide cybersecurity under the Office of Cybersecurity
initiatives.summary: New Mexico's Cybersecurity Act stood up an independent Office of Cybersecurity whose CISO reports to a Cybersecurity Advisory Committee, with scope extended to local, county, tribal, and education entities. The state expects roughly $13M over four years via the State and Local Cybersecurity Grant Program (80% to locals) and is pursuing statewide procurement vehicles for shared security tooling.

initiatives.name: Enterprise services & infrastructure modernization
initiatives.summary: DoIT's Enterprise Services program is the core motion for Dell: consolidated hosting, network, cloud, and the equipment-replacement refresh cycle for aging state infrastructure. The 5% FY27 rate increase signals continued reinvestment in the shared estate.

initiatives.name: Digital Trunked Radio System (DTRS) expansion
initiatives.summary: Secretary Barreras has named expansion of the statewide public-safety radio system (DTRS) a top priority to improve coverage and interoperability for federal, state, local, and tribal users — a sustained capital and infrastructure track adjacent to DoIT's core IT services.

procurement.intro: New Mexico buying runs through two layers: the General Services Department (GSD) State Purchasing Division, which establishes statewide price agreements open to state and local government entities, and DoIT, which reviews IT projects and professional-services procurement. Confirm Dell's coverage on the relevant GSD statewide price agreements and understand where a deal needs DoIT's IT review.

vehicles.name: GSD Statewide Price Agreements
vehicles.detail: The State Purchasing Division (GSD) establishes statewide pricing agreements usable by NM governmental entities; the state notes over $6B expended annually across these and cooperative contracts. The primary path to pre-competed access — confirm Dell hardware/services coverage.

vehicles.name: DoIT IT review & professional-services route
vehicles.detail: Goods and non-professional services generally go through GSD; professional services and IT projects route through DFA/DoIT, with DoIT IT project review and a Contract Review Board in the loop for technology buys.

vehicles.name: Formal solicitations (>$60K)
vehicles.detail: Procurements above the $60K threshold require formal sealed bids/proposals under the State Procurement Code; below it, agencies use quote-based methods. Scope deal size against these thresholds early.

powerStructure.name: Manny Barreras
powerStructure.title: Cabinet Secretary & State CIO
powerStructure.note: Senate-confirmed March 2025 (appointed Sept 2024); former Motorola Solutions account executive. Top relationship for the statewide IT and DTRS radio motion.

powerStructure.name: Jason Habedank
powerStructure.title: Deputy Cabinet Secretary
powerStructure.note: Joined Feb 2024 with 25+ years of IT and leadership experience; key operational counterpart.

powerStructure.name: Raja Sambandam
powerStructure.title: State CISO (Office of Cybersecurity)
powerStructure.note: Reports to the Cybersecurity Advisory Committee, not the CIO, with statewide (incl. local/tribal) scope. Owner of the whole-of-state security buying center.

powerStructure.name: John Padilla
powerStructure.title: Director, Enterprise Services
powerStructure.note: Owns the consolidated hosting/network/cloud and equipment-refresh buying center — the core DoIT infrastructure motion.

powerStructure.name: Jeff Lopez
powerStructure.title: Director, Office of Broadband Access & Expansion
powerStructure.note: Runs the $382M BEAD program and grant awards — the broadband/middle-mile relationship.

news.title: DoIT proposes first rewrite of IT project oversight & certification rules since 2005
news.impact: Notice of Proposed Rulemaking repeals/replaces 1.12.5 NMAC (Oversight of IT Projects) and 1.12.9 NMAC (Project Certification), removing the defunct IT Commission and modernizing project plans, risk assessments, and phased funding tied to certification milestones. Reshapes the DoIT IT-review gate that Dell deals route through — watch the final rules for changed thresholds and approval steps.

news.title: State fully funds DTRS public-safety radio subscriber fees through June 2027
news.impact: DoIT has covered DTRS subscriber fees since 2024 and, with new legislative funding, will do so through FY27; the system spans 100+ tower sites, ~80% statewide coverage, and 23,274 subscribers across 90+ agencies. DoIT is now working on a long-term sustainable funding plan beyond FY27 — a sustained public-safety infrastructure track.

news.title: NTIA approves New Mexico's $382M BEAD broadband plan
news.impact: 31 grants to connect 42,500+ locations across 32 of 33 counties; construction follows contract execution by OBAE. Network/edge opportunity, not a DoIT data-center buy — map the grantees.

news.title: DoIT submits FY27 appropriation request with 5% Enterprise Services rate increase
news.impact: Confirms continued reinvestment in the shared infrastructure estate; agency IT bills rise, reinforcing the enterprise-services motion.

news.title: Senate unanimously confirms Manny Barreras as DoIT Secretary & State CIO
news.impact: Stable top leadership; priorities center on modernizing/securing state IT and expanding the DTRS public-safety radio system.

news.title: Governor appoints Manny Barreras Cabinet Secretary and State CIO
news.impact: Vendor-background leader installed at the top of the statewide IT motion — relationship reset point.

dellActions: Confirm Dell coverage on the relevant GSD statewide price agreements — that, plus DoIT's IT review path, is the access route to the New Mexico public sector.
Lead the Enterprise Services / Equipment Replacement refresh with ISG (server, storage, network) and tie messaging to the FY27 rate-supported reinvestment in the shared estate.
Engage the Office of Cybersecurity (CISO Sambandam) on whole-of-state shared security tooling, leveraging the SLCGP grant economics and statewide procurement vehicles.
Position network/edge and middle-mile infrastructure around the OBAE $382M BEAD buildout by mapping the 31 grantees rather than chasing OBAE as a direct buyer.
Build the DTRS/public-safety angle with Secretary Barreras as a sustained infrastructure track adjacent to core IT.
